schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch contrast Kings, which only notes the war, with Chronicles' full narrative of a great campaign in which Abijah, helped by the Lord, crushed Israel; the regnal date matches 1 Kings 15:1.
Matthew Henry weighs the warrant for war: God gave Abijah leave (unlike Rehoboam, 2 Chron 11:4) because Jeroboam was now the aggressor and had revealed himself by the calves and cast-off priests.
Clarke lays out the chapter's whole course in advance.
Gill fixes the accession date; Ellicott notes 'Now' is absent in Hebrew and the verse nearly matches its Kings parallel.

themes: who holds the kingdom of the LORD

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil take 'Michaiah' as a scribal corruption of Maachah (so LXX, Syriac, Arabic); Maachah was granddaughter of Absalom via Tamar, and Uriel of Gibeah her husband; the war-notice is drawn from 1 Kings 15:6 with its 'all his days' clause omitted.
Matthew Henry harmonizes the names by descent (Absalom via Tamar) and immediate parentage (Uriel) rather than by emendation.
Clarke refers the reader back to his note at 2 Chron 11:20.

debates.question: How are the two names given to Abijah's mother to be reconciled?

sides.position: 'Michaiah' is a scribal corruption of 'Maachah'; she was granddaughter of Absalom through Tamar, wife of Uriel
she is called daughter of Absalom by descent and daughter of Uriel by immediate parentage, without corruption

schools.summary: Clarke judges the numbers overcharged by a cipher, marshalling Syriac, Arabic, and many Vulgate editions for the smaller figures (40,000 / 80,000 / 50,000).

Keil and Ellicott defend the received text: the figures agree closely with David's census totals in 2 Sam 24:9 and 1 Chron 21:5, making corruption improbable; 'set the battle in array' = began the battle.
Henry lets the disproportion (ten tribes to two) teach that the battle is not always to the strong; the smaller army had God on its side.

debates.question: Are the army numbers (400,000 / 800,000) reliable or corrupted?

sides.position: overcharged by a cipher; read forty and eighty thousand, with the versions and older editions
reliable, agreeing with the census totals of 2 Sam 24:9 and 1 Chron 21:5

schools.summary: Keil places Mount Zemaraim east of Bethel near the border (with Robinson); Ellicott hears the Chronicler's hand in 'from upon the mount' and compares Jotham's address from Gerizim (Judges 9:7).
Gill derives the name from a Benjamite city within Ephraim's borders and infers Abijah spoke under a granted parley.
Clarke (with Targum and Jarchi) takes it for a height on Ephraim's confines, distinct from Samaria's hill.
Henry: try reason before force; war is the last resort of kings, and 'how forcible are right words.'

Ellicott reads the rhetorical 'is it not to you to know?' and expounds the covenant of salt as a firm, unalterable compact ratified by salt at meals and sacrifices (Lev 2:13; Num 18:19); its antiquity supports the speech's authenticity. Keil: an irrevocable covenant.
Clarke cites the Targum ('as the sea's waters never grow sweet') and Num 18:19; Wesley: 'a perpetual covenant.'
Henry: Abijah claims a divine right, the crown entailed on David's house by a lasting covenant made by salted sacrifice (with bishop Patrick).
Gill: Abijah names Jeroboam 'servant of Solomon' in contempt, once the mere receiver of Solomon's tax in Ephraim.
Ellicott anchors the rebellion in 1 Kings 11:26; Keil reads the verse as exposing that the whole northern polity rests on a servant's revolt propped up by worthless men.
Henry stresses the dishonesty and ingratitude: Jeroboam rebelled against the lord who had preferred him (1 Kings 11:28), preying on Rehoboam's weakness.

themes: the crooked foundation of the north

schools.summary: Ellicott glosses 'vain men' (as of Abimelech's and Jephthah's followers, Judges 9:4; 11:3) and calls 'young and tenderhearted' inexact, since Rehoboam was forty-one (2 Chron 12:13) and elsewhere haughty; Abijah overstates to spare his father.
Clarke draws the reverse inference: since Rehoboam is called young and tender-hearted, he cannot really have been forty-one; and 'children of Belial' are men of abandoned principle.
Gill: the 'vain men' were void of the fear of God.
Henry: unsteady men given to change, for shaking off the yoke of government.

debates.question: How is 'young and tenderhearted' to be squared with Rehoboam's being forty-one?

sides.position: the epithet is Abijah's rhetorical overstatement; Rehoboam was indeed forty-one
the epithet is accurate, so Rehoboam cannot really have been forty-one at his accession

themes: vain men and sons of Belial

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'the kingdom in the hand of the sons of David' is the kingdom Jehovah holds through David's house; the calves are Jeroboam-made idols (cf. Isa 44:9) and no ground of confidence. Keil: their trust in multitude and gods is vain while they have cast out the priests.
Wesley: the calves are 'that among you which may damp your confidence' — images God abhors.
Henry: the calves are Achans, troublers of the camp, that will make the true God oppose them.

Ellicott and Keil expound 'to fill the hand' as the legal idiom for instituting a priest (Exod 28:41; 29:1ff.); priests 'after the manner of the nations' are drawn from all classes, not Levi (1 Kings 12:31); 'no-gods' formed like Deut 32:17, the calves being mere idols.
Gill: Jeroboam expelled the lawful priests so they would not teach true worship, and to keep the tithes.
Clarke: a bullock and seven rams was the price of the spurious priesthood; Wesley: 'to fill the hand' is to make oneself a priest.
Henry: any man who would pay could be a priest — priests that were no priests for gods that were no gods.

Keil: Judah has not forsaken the LORD in observing the legal worship, sons of Aaron as priests and Levites in service; Ellicott harmonizes the claim with 1 Kings 15:3 — it concerns the official worship, not the king's personal piety.
Clarke: Judah still served the LORD by his own law; Wesley: 'the LORD' is Jehovah, the only true God, whose worship Judah maintained.
Gill: they own no God but the one living and true God, not the calves of Dan and Bethel.
Henry: Abijah, not himself truly religious, boasts of the form of godliness while valuing in his people a piety he lacked.

outliers.note: Observes that those who deny the power of godliness often boast of its form; Abijah pleads the cause of his kingdom's religion though his own heart was not right.

schools.summary: Ellicott anchors each rite in the law (Exod 29:38; 30:7; Lev 24:5) as 'keeping the charge' (Lev 8:35), and notes only one table and candlestick here though 2 Chron 4:7 has ten of each.
Wesley explains the singular 'table' and 'candlestick' (only one used at a time), notes the pure gold of Exod 25:23, and suspects Abijah trusted external worship to atone for his life.
Gill: the priests offered burnt sacrifice and incense on their several altars, morning and evening.
Henry: the temple service and furniture are of God's own appointing, abided by without adding or diminishing.

outliers.note: Suspects Abijah flattered himself that keeping up the external worship would make satisfaction for the errors of his life.

themes: keeping the charge of the LORD
one table and candlestick vs. ten

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: at Judah's head are the true God and his priests with the alarm-trumpets, the divinely appointed pledge that God would remember his people in war (Num 10:9; 31:6).
Clarke contrasts the calves with the living Jehovah and reads the trumpets as a threat of coming vengeance; Wesley: to fight the God of the fathers is folly, treachery, and ingratitude.
Henry: nothing so emboldens men as assurance that God is with them; the trumpets are a martial and sacred sign of his presence.
Gill: God is present as captain, to go before Judah's armies and fight their battles.

themes: fighting the God of the fathers

schools.summary: Keil details the manoeuvre: the ambush circles to Judah's rear, so the men of Judah, turning, see war before and behind, and cry to the LORD as the priests blow.

Henry reads the ambush as the measure of Jeroboam: he answered the speech with a stratagem, laying an ambush during a parley against all the laws of arms — Abijah was for peace, but they were for war (Ps 120:7).
Clarke: the flanking party would have proved fatal had not the LORD interposed; Wesley: Jeroboam trusted the longest sword, not the better cause.
Gill: while Abijah spoke, Jeroboam detached a party to lie in ambush behind him.

Ellicott compares the ambuscade at Ai (Josh 8:20) and Gibeah and corrects 'looked back' (they perceived, not were unprepared); Keil: in the strait they cry to the LORD, the priests trumpet, and God smites the enemy.

Henry makes the verse a treatise on prayer in extremity: no ambush can cut off communication with heaven; God brings his people into straits to teach them to cry, and earnest praying is crying (Ps 118:10).
Gill: Judah looked back on hearing the noise behind them.

themes: communication with heaven never cut off

schools.summary: Ellicott: the war-cry, raised after the priests' blast (cf. Gideon, Judges 7:18), and 'God smote' describes the wild panic of the host (same phrase in Judges 20:35; 2 Chron 14:12). Keil: as they shouted, God smote Jeroboam and all Israel.
Wesley: to the cry of prayer Judah added the shout of faith, and became more than conquerors.
Gill: the shout heartened Judah and dismayed the enemy.
Keil: Israel fled and God gave them into Judah's hand, so that Abijah inflicted a defeat costing more than half Jeroboam's army.
Gill: the host was in such consternation they could not stand their ground but took to flight.

Henry: God smote them with terror so they could not strike a stroke — the battle was the LORD's; yet he laments the sad effect of division, Israelite blood shed by Israelites while the heathen cried 'Aha!'

themes: God delivered them into their hand

schools.summary: Ellicott ('a great smiting,' Num 11:33) insists on a single decisive battle and defends the number as the contemporaries' expression of a total defeat (with Keil), noting the Syriac's 'five thousand' and rejecting Reuss.
Clarke again finds 500,000 incredible and would read fifty thousand, per his argument at v.3 that the numbers are overcharged.
Henry accepts the figure as a wonder of judgment — more than ever slain in one battle — but the battle was the LORD's.

debates.question: Is the figure of 500,000 slain literal or exaggerated?

sides.position: a copyist's or contemporaries' swelling; perhaps read fifty thousand
retained as the contemporaries' numerical expression of a total defeat

themes: a ruin only God could work

schools.summary: Ellicott fixes the vocabulary ('brought under' = bowed down, 2 Chron 12:6; 'relied upon' = leaned, Isa 10:20); Keil reads the clauses as cause and effect — Judah grew strong because they relied on Jahve.
Clarke cites the Targum: they prevailed because they depended on the Word of the God of their fathers.
Henry: reliance and the prayer of faith are the prevailing power (1 John 5:4); yet the victory was restrained short of reducing the north, by God's determinate counsel.
Gill: Israel was humbled but not brought back under David's house.

themes: prevailing by reliance on the LORD
the theological hinge of the chapter

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott identify Bethel (Beitin, Josh 7:2; Gen 12:8), the unlocated Jeshanah, and Ephron/Ephrain (not Josh 15:9's southern Ephron, but perhaps Ophrah of Judges 6:11 or the Ephraim of John 11:54); Ellicott notes the cities were held only briefly, recovered by Baasha (1 Kings 15:17).
Gill: Abijah pursued as Jeroboam fled and took the cities.

outliers.note: Records the Targum's rendering of Bethel as 'Bethlehem,' and confesses that the sites of Jeshanah and its neighbours are unknown.

themes: Bethel, seat of a golden calf

schools.summary: Keil is inclined to read the blow as the prophecy against Jeroboam's house and the death of his son (1 Kings 14:1); Ellicott resists this, taking it as a judicial visitation at his actual death (cf. 1 Sam 25:38), not the war nor the son's death.
Clarke reports the unsettled question of who died and sides with Jeroboam, struck now and dying two years after Abijah (1 Kings 14:20; 15:9); Wesley: he escaped Abijah's sword but not God's.
Gill: Jeroboam could no longer raise an army against Abijah.

debates.question: What was the blow by which the LORD struck Jeroboam?

sides.position: the prophecy against his house and the death of his son (1 Kings 14)
a judicial visitation at his own death, not the war nor the son's death

outliers.note: Raises the unsettled question whether the one struck and killed is Abijah or Jeroboam, siding with the prevailing view that Jeroboam is meant.

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott read Abijah's establishment and large family as the counterpart to Jeroboam's decline; since he reigned only three years, most wives and children preceded his accession (2 Chron 11:21), and the fruitfulness contrasts with Jeroboam's soon-extirpated house.
Gill: 'waxed mighty' in riches, power, and family.
Clarke supposes the fourteen marriages were alliances with neighbouring powers.
Henry hears first a note of blessing ('quiver full') but ends on the sober cutting-off of Abijah's months, death conquering the conqueror.

outliers.note: Reads the fourteen marriages as political alliances with neighbouring powers.

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the alliteration of 'his ways and his sayings' and that 'story' renders midrash; Keil identifies the midrash of Iddo as the source and ties the following 'rest' (2 Chron 14:1) to Abijah's victory.
Clarke: 'story' is midrash, 'commentary' — the first such mention; Iddo's commentary is lost, but the chapter's speech shows Abijah an able speaker.
Gill: the record kept Abijah's remarkable sayings, for he was a man of wisdom and eloquence.
Henry: a man of his own ways and sayings, yet his months were soon cut off.

themes: the reign closed in a formula
